name: Gillette Stadium
tagline: Home of the Patriots dynasty, crowned by a 218-foot lighthouse, hosting Scotland's Tartan Army and a quarterfinal.

why.title: The Lighthouse
why.text: A 218-foot tower inspired by New England's maritime heritage anchors the north end, with a 360-degree observation deck and the largest outdoor curved video board in America right beside it.

why.title: Tartan Army Invasion
why.text: Scotland plays two group matches here, and thousands of kilted supporters are basing in Providence and Boston. It's the marquee fan story of the venue.

why.title: Turf Ripped Out for Grass
why.text: Crews tore out nearly 20 years of FieldTurf and built a sand-based root zone with real Kentucky bluegrass sod, so the ball rolls slower and truer than it ever has here.

why.title: A Quarterfinal Stage
why.text: Beyond five group games and a Round of 32, Boston Stadium hosts a July 9 quarterfinal, one of just eight in the entire tournament.

gettingThere: The stadium sits in Foxborough, a transit island about 25 miles from both Boston and Providence, with no everyday train service. On every match day the MBTA runs dedicated express Boston Stadium Trains from South Station straight to Foxboro Station, an 8-minute walk from the gates.
transit: Boston Stadium Trains run non-stop from South Station to Foxboro on all 7 match days, a ~1-hour ride; buy the $80 round-trip ONLY in the MBTA mTicket app.
Last inbound train arrives ~90 min before kickoff; return trains start ~30 min after the whistle and leave every ~15 min until cleared.
Driving is ~45 min from Boston or ~30-40 min from Providence, but lots fill fast and exit gridlock is notorious.
Flying in: Boston Logan (BOS) is ~27 mi; T.F. Green in Providence (PVD) is ~18 mi and often cheaper for southbound fans.
matchday: There's no fan district at the stadium itself, just Patriot Place, the open-air dining and entertainment complex wrapped around it. The real party is in downtown Boston (City Hall Plaza Fan Festival, June 12-27) and Providence, where Scotland fans gather before busing or training in.

food.name: Smokehouse Grill
food.note: house-smoked brisket and Buffalo wings done right
why: House-smoked brisket, not reheated stadium fare
Right by the Lighthouse end gates

food.name: Italian Sausage stand
food.note: sausage with grilled peppers and onions, classic
why: New England gameday classic, peppers and onions
Found on every main concourse

food.name: Papa Gino's / Stadium Pizza
food.note: local New England pizza chain and fried dough
why: Homegrown New England pizza chain
Get the fried dough for dessert

food.name: The Taproom
food.note: dozens of local craft beers on tap
why: Dozens of local craft beers on tap
Beer-hall vibe over standard kiosk lines

pre.name: FIFA Fan Festival - Boston
pre.where: Boston City Hall Plaza, 1 City Hall Square, downtown Boston; ~26 mi north of Gillette in Foxborough. Take the MBTA Boston Stadium Train back from South Station (Blue/Green Line to Gov't Center, steps away)
pre.note: free official FIFA fan zone, June 12-27, with daily live match broadcasts and a local cultural showcase; free but advance registration required
why: Official free fan zone, big-screen match broadcasts
Downtown hub before training out to Foxborough

pre.name: CBS Sporting Club
pre.where: 200 Patriot Pl, steps from gates (former CBS Scene space)
pre.note: three-story sports bar with a 25-ft LED wall and stadium views, packed on gamedays
why: Giant LED wall, closest big-screen scene to the gates
Three-story sports bar literally steps from the stadium

pre.name: The Harp
pre.note: Boston bar with patio overlooking the stadium
why: Boston-institution bar with stadium-view patio
Three-minute walk from the gates

pre.name: Davio's Northern Italian Steakhouse
pre.note: upscale steakhouse, lively bar, reservations smart
why: Upscale steakhouse for a proper pre-match meal
Lively bar, but book ahead

pre.name: Six String Grill & Stage
pre.note: live music, big screens, craft and domestic beers
why: Live music builds the matchday energy
Big screens and beers steps from gates

post.name: The Harp
post.note: seasonal patio, local beers, post-game crowd buzz
why: Seasonal patio catches the post-game crowd buzz
Local beers, three minutes from gates

post.name: Howl Splitsville Golf Suites
post.note: bowling, Full Swing golf suites, live bands, late-night drinks and food
why: Bowling and golf suites kill the exit gridlock
Live bands and late-night drinks

post.name: Bar Louie
post.note: casual gastrobar, cocktails, easy wind-down spot
why: Casual gastrobar for an easy wind-down
Cocktails without the crush

post.name: Tavolino
post.note: Italian bar and dining, calmer late-evening vibe
why: Calmer Italian spot for late-evening dining
Wait out the lot gridlock here

merch.name: Patriots ProShop
merch.where: Gillette Stadium complex, Foxborough (370 Patriot Pl; temporarily relocated to South Marketplace, next to Petco, during the World Cup)

merch.name: Patriots Hall of Fame (museum + gift shop)
merch.where: 2 Patriot Pl, adjacent to the ProShop; note: the Hall is CLOSED May 14-July 2026 during the World Cup

food: Patriot Place: restaurants, bars and a cinema right outside the gates, far better than concourse lines.
Boston classics: lobster rolls, clam chowder ('chowdah') and oysters are the local must-eat canon.
North End: Boston's Italian neighborhood for the best pre-match pasta and cannoli in the city.
Faneuil Hall / Quincy Market and the Seaport: the pub-dense zones where fans pack in to watch.
tips: Buy the $80 Boston Stadium Train ticket EARLY in the mTicket app; it's the best way in and includes the return. R32/QF train tickets drop later, so set a reminder.
Arrive 2+ hours pre-kickoff: the last train lands ~90 min out and security plus the 8-min walk eat into that window.
Be patient on the way out; return trains clear the queue every ~15 min and beat driving out of the lots every time.
Best atmosphere is the north (Lighthouse) end by the giant video board; mid-level sideline 100s-200s give the cleanest sight lines.
Clear-bag policy is enforced: clear bags up to 12x6x12 or a small clutch up to 4.5x6.5x2; lockers handle non-compliant bags.
Stay in Providence to save money (Scotland fans proved it), or Boston for the Fan Festival and nightlife; match lodging to your priority.
weather: Open-air with no roof and mild for a US venue: June highs ~76F and the July 9 QF ~85F, so pack sunscreen, a hat and a rain shell for afternoon thunderstorms.
lore: Ground zero for the Patriots dynasty: the Brady-Belichick era and multiple Super Bowl runs launched from here.
Built entirely with private money by owner Robert Kraft for ~$325M, opening in 2002 as the first major Boston-area stadium in 100 years.
A serious soccer venue already: it staged Copa America Centenario 2016, Copa America 2024 and Gold Cup rounds, and holds a 65,612 soccer-crowd record.
A premier concert stop with a Taylor Swift bond: she's played here 13 times, the only venue she's hit on every headlining tour.

transport.summary: Gillette is a transit island in Foxborough with no everyday rail service, so for the World Cup the MBTA and Boston Host Committee run match-day-only Boston Stadium Trains and Express Buses. Organizers expect ~70% of fans to arrive by public transit and explicitly warn against walking or biking the route.

rail.name: Boston Stadium Train (MBTA)
rail.detail: 14 express, non-stop trains run on each of the 7 match days, a ~1-hour ride to Foxboro Station, an under-10-min walk from the gates. Last inbound train arrives ~90 min before kickoff; return trains leave every ~15 min starting ~30 min after the final whistle. Fully accessible; buy ONLY in the MBTA mTicket app ('Buy Tickets to Boston Stadium') and you must hold a same-day match ticket.
rail.deal: $80 round-trip, also valid on all Commuter Rail trains that day

points.t: last inbound train lands ~90 min pre-kickoff

bus.name: Boston Stadium Express
bus.detail: Official Host Committee coach service with 20+ pickup points at hotels across Greater Boston plus Logan Airport and the Rhode Island Convention Center in Providence. Departs 3-4.5 hrs before kickoff, returns starting 30 min after the whistle, drops within a 10-min walk; over half the fleet is ADA accessible. Advance booking only at bostonstadiumexpress.com, same-day match ticket required.

points.t: over half the fleet ADA accessible

shuttle.name: South Station connections
shuttle.detail: At South Station, transfer to the Red Line, Silver Line and Commuter Rail; subway and select bus routes run until ~2 AM after weekday matches and ~4 AM after the Sat Jun 13 night match.

points.t: Red Line, Silver Line, Commuter Rail
service to ~4 AM after the night match

transport.fromAirport: Boston Logan (BOS) is ~27 mi and a Boston Stadium Express pickup point; T.F. Green / Providence (PVD) is ~18 mi and often cheaper for southbound fans, with Express buses from the nearby RI Convention Center. From downtown Boston, take the train from South Station; from Providence, take an Express bus.
transport.rideshare: Designated rideshare drop-off and pickup zones are in Lot 16 (next to Bass Pro Shops) and Lot 15 (near the CVS Health Gate); a post-match on-demand rideshare shuttle is also expected. Surge pricing is severe, with normal $50-80 fares pushing to $100-200+ on match days, and the same exit gridlock as driving.
transport.bike: Not recommended. Organizers explicitly warn against walking or cycling along Route 1 to the stadium, describing the route as unsafe.
tips: Buy the $80 train ticket EARLY in the mTicket app; it includes the return and is valid on all Commuter Rail that day. R32/QF tickets release closer to those dates, so set a reminder.

From Providence (the Scotland base), the $95 Boston Stadium Express from the RI Convention Center is the cleanest option; ~1,100 Tartan Army fans instead chartered yellow school buses at ~$47/person to save money.
The train and Express bus require a same-day match ticket to purchase, and both sell out; do not assume walk-up transit exists.
Be patient on the way out: return trains clear the queue every ~15 min and beat driving out of the lots every time.

parking.summary: Classic suburban big-lot setup with ~10,000+ spaces, but for the World Cup ALL parking is ticket-holder-only and strictly prepaid, with no walk-up or cash parking on match day. Lots open ~4 hours before kickoff.

lots.name: Official stadium lots (JustPark)
lots.detail: ~5,000 FIFA-managed spaces sold in advance only through JustPark (justpark.com), FIFA's official parking partner. Group-stage prices ran ~$175-600 per spot, with knockout games higher. Over-sized/RV parking is available via advance booking.

lots.name: Satellite lots along Route 1
lots.detail: 5,000+ additional spaces in independent lots lining Route 1 at varying, often cheaper rates; book ahead and expect notorious post-match exit congestion.

parking.prepaid: Mandatory. All match-day parking is prepaid and ticket-holder-only; official lots are bought in advance via JustPark. There is no cash or walk-up parking, and non-ticketed fans cannot enter the lots ('If you don't have a ticket, don't come to Gillette').
parking.accessible: Accessible parking is available near stadium entrances; book the ADA option in advance through JustPark. Wheelchair-accessible taxis and MBTA paratransit (to South Station) are also available. Accessibility line: (800) 543-1776.
parking.tailgating: Permitted for valid match-ticket holders only, in line with the prepaid lot rules; the stadium-side lots open ~4 hours before kickoff. Patriot Place dining and bars sit right outside if you'd rather skip the lot scene.
tips: Reserve a JustPark spot well ahead; with no walk-up parking, arriving without a prepaid pass means no entry.
Driving from Boston is ~45 min and Providence ~30-40 min, but lots clog and exit gridlock is famous, so the train usually wins overall.
Satellite Route 1 lots can be cheaper than the $175+ official spots if you book early.
Do NOT plan to be dropped off and walk in along Route 1; organizers call that route unsafe.
